assignment.name: vague
assignment.title: Make something cool with AI
assignment.description: Build a project that uses AI in some interesting way. The exact scope, audience, and form of the deliverable are up to you.

verdict.notes: This plan is unfocused and lacks a systems modeling anchor. It lists three separate generative AI applications without committing to a single, coherent project—and none of them involve the kind of dynamic modeling, feedback loops, or emergent behavior that Anand evaluates. A creative tool is fine as a capstone, but this reads as 'I'll build an AI wrapper' rather than 'I'll model a system and use AI as a lens.' The 14-day window doesn't save a plan that has no clear system to understand.

history: Text generation (e.g., creative writing, chatbot, summarizer)

verdict.notes: This is not a plan—it's a list of possible ideas with no commitment, no modeling framework, and no systems thinking. You've listed three entirely different directions (generative content broadly, text generation specifically, then chatbots) without choosing one or explaining why. Anand's assignment asks you to *build* something with AI; your response shows you haven't yet decided what you're building, how you'll evaluate it, or what feedback loops or emergent behavior you're studying. Pick a concrete domain and a single approach (CLD or ABM preferred; if it's a chatbot, define the system you're modeling—e.g., how does user engagement or knowledge retention emerge from prompt design choices?). Right now this is a brainstorm, not a project plan.

verdict.notes: This is a brainstorm list, not a project plan. There's no commitment to a single direction, no modeling approach, no tool choice, and no clear deliverable. Anand will reject this immediately for lacking the specificity needed to evaluate feasibility or scope. Pick one idea, anchor it in a real system (e.g., a specific student cohort, a real coding curriculum), and commit to a modeling method—whether that's a CLD of tutor-student feedback loops, an ABM of learner agents with heterogeneous prior knowledge, or a deployed prototype with evaluation metrics. As written, this reads like brainstorming, not a plan.

verdict.notes: This plan is a template, not a project. It names three *categories of work* (analysis, content analysis, dataset) without specifying a domain, a concrete problem, a modeling approach, or success criteria. Anand will read this as 'I haven't decided what to build yet'—which leaves the actual scope, feasibility, and quality completely unknown. Pick a real dataset and a real question (e.g., 'sentiment analysis of customer churn predictors in SaaS reviews' or 'toxicity detection in gaming forum moderation'), commit to a specific model class (classification, clustering, LLM-based extraction), and show how you'll validate it. Right now this reads like a brainstorm, not a plan.

branches.option: Interactive tool (chatbot, tutor, game, assistant)

verdict.notes: This plan confuses scope with options. You've listed three different project ideas without committing to one, which signals either unclear thinking or an attempt to hedge—both are red flags. A SQL query generator is feasible in 14 days, but it's a narrow, well-trodden application (LLMs → SQL is commodity tooling) with no systems thinking component. You need to pick ONE direction, define your actual model or system boundary, and explain what makes it 'cool' beyond 'uses an LLM.' For Anand's rubric, this plan lacks conceptual rigor: there's no modeling (CLD, ABM, or formal system), no scenario comparison, and no evidence you've thought deeply about failure modes, responsible AI use, or emergent behavior.
